coredata.dc:title: Bio-derived graphitic carbon quantum dot encapsulated S- and N-doped graphene sheets with unusual battery-type behavior for high-performance supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Pallavolu, Mohan Reddy
Prabhu, S.
Nallapureddy, Ramesh Reddy
Kumar, Arla Sai
Banerjee, Arghya Narayan
Joo, Sang Woo
coredata.dc:description: Electrochemical supercapacitors (ECs) have increased large attention in recent years due to their faster charging and discharging process, large specific capacity, and high power density. Herein, we designed the preparation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and N-/S-doped CQDs, encapsulated within graphene sheets via a lemon-juice-derived sustainable route for high-performance positive electrode material in supercapacitor application. The as-prepared electrodes revealed an unusual battery-type behavior due to the presence of large number of O-/N-/S-containing functional groups within the graphitic network of the CQDs-graphene composite, which enhances the redox-active sites. The specific capacities were obtained for CQDs, N-doped, and S-doped CQDs at 284, 181, and 125 mAh/g at 1 A/g current density. The S-doped CQDs delivered 94.5% capacitance retention over 10,000 cycles with 98% Coulombic efficiency. The real-time symmetric supercapacitor was constructed using as-prepared S-doped CQDs with an operated potential window of 1.4 V. The constructed symmetric supercapacitor shows high specific capacity, energy, and power densities of 21.7 mAh/g (55.7 F/g at 1 A/g), 15.2 Wh/kg, and 700 W/kg, respectively. The outstanding electrochemical results are based on the easy formation of heteroatom doped S and N-CQDs to enhance the better electronic/ionic motion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-S-CQDs
Bio-derived sustainable synthesis
Battery-type behavior
Superior energy-power performance
Symmetric supercapacitors

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and testing of symmetric supercapacitor (S-CQDs//S-CQDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of synthesis process bio-derived S, N-doped CQDs encapsulated by graphene sheets. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectra of CQDs, N-CQDs, and S-CQDs. XPS results of CQDs, N-CQDs, S-CQDs (c) survey spectrum, (d) deconvoluted spectrum of C 1s, (e) N 1s, (f) S 2p, and (g) O 1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TEM and HR-TEM images of CQDs (a, b, and c), N-CQDs (d, e, and f), and S-CQDs (g, h, and i), respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) comparative Cyclic voltammetry profiles of CQD, N-CQD, and S-CQDs, Cyclic voltammetry, and Galvanostatic charge-discharge results of as-synthesized (b and e) CQDs, (c and f) N-CQDs, (d and g) S-CQDs, (h) Specific capacity vs. current density of CQDs, N-CQDs, and S-CQDs, (i) Nyquist plot of the CQDs, N-CQDs, and S-CQDs (inset: equivalent circuit), (j) Magnified version at high-frequency region, (k) Stability performance of as-prepared S-CQDs (inset: Schematic structure of the molecular model of the CQD encapsulated N-/S-doped graphene). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Schematic illustration of the S-CQDs//S-CQDs symmetric supercapacitors, (b) CV curves at different voltages with a constant scanning rate of 20 mV s−1, (c) CV curve of symmetric supercapacitors at different scanning rates, (d) GCD curves of the S-CQDs//S-CQDs symmetric supercapacitor at different voltage with a constant current density of 6 Ag-1, (e) GCD curves of the S-CQDs//S-CQDs symmetric supercapacitors with various current densities between 0.5 and 10 Ag-1, (f) Specific capacity of the symmetric supercapacitor at different current densities, (g) Nyquist plot of the symmetric supercapacitor (inset: same at high-to-mid frequency region, and the equivalent circuit) (h) Ragone plot of energy density versus power density of symmetric supercapacitor compared with similar reported devices, and (i) life cyclic stability (inset: four 1.4 V symmetric supercapacitor connected to series to power up 50 red LEDs with an operating potential window of 4.2 V). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Possible reversible redox reactions of various functional groups within N-/S-CQDs-graphene composite electrodes in the aqueous electrolyte during electrochemical characterization [46–51].
Elemental composition of CQD, N-CQD, and S-CQDs.

head.ce:title: Bio-derived graphitic carbon quantum dot encapsulated S- and N-doped graphene sheets with unusual battery-type behavior for high-performance supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan, 38541, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Periyar University, Salem, 636011, Tamilnadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Periyar University, Salem 636011, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan, 38541, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemical supercapacitors (ECs) have increased large attention in recent years due to their faster charging and discharging process, large specific capacity, and high power density. Herein, we designed the preparation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and N-/S-doped CQDs, encapsulated within graphene sheets via a lemon-juice-derived sustainable route for high-performance positive electrode material in supercapacitor application. The as-prepared electrodes revealed an unusual battery-type behavior due to the presence of large number of O-/N-/S-containing functional groups within the graphitic network of the CQDs-graphene composite, which enhances the redox-active sites. The specific capacities were obtained for CQDs, N-doped, and S-doped CQDs at 284, 181, and 125 mAh/g at 1 A/g current density. The S-doped CQDs delivered 94.5% capacitance retention over 10,000 cycles with 98% Coulombic efficiency. The real-time symmetric supercapacitor was constructed using as-prepared S-doped CQDs with an operated potential window of 1.4 V. The constructed symmetric supercapacitor shows high specific capacity, energy, and power densities of 21.7 mAh/g (55.7 F/g at 1 A/g), 15.2 Wh/kg, and 700 W/kg, respectively. The outstanding electrochemical results are based on the easy formation of heteroatom doped S and N-CQDs to enhance the better electronic/ionic motion.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, substantial research has been concentrated on innovative materials for energy storage. In energy storage devices, supercapacitor devices have increased much consideration due to its fast and reversible redox reactions and high theoretical capacities [1,2]. Supercapacitors are three types such as pseudocapacitors (PSC), electric double-layer capacitors (EDLC), and hybrid capacitors [3]. EDLCs have a long cycle lifespan, a high energy-density and reasonable power-density, a fast charge/discharge rate with a high retention rate, have no chemical or structural changes during charge storage, can work in extreme temperatures, and are very safe, although their energy-densities are still lesser than that of mid-level batteries and the relative manufacturing cost is still an issue. On the other hand, in PSCs, the charge transfer occurs through surface Faradaic, redox reactions, where electrolyte ions diffuse into pores and undergo fast, reversible surface reactions, and therefore, the charge is stored electrochemically. Although PSCs possess high theoretical capacitance, an increase in the specific energy density, leads to a decrease in the power density and lifetime of the cell during the redox process, thus restricting their applications [5]. Therefore, a combination of both the features of EDLCs and PSCs into a single system to fabricate hybrid supercapacitors can attain a very high energy density without compromising the power density, so also, not losing the cycle lifetime and affordability. Generally, there are three types of hybrid supercapacitors. In one case, one of the electrodes of the two-electrode device is composed of EDLC material, whilst the other is made up of PSC material. In another variation, either or both the electrodes are composed of a combination of EDLC + PSC composite materials. And in another case, one electrode is made up of a battery-type material, while the other can be an EDLC or PSC, or both (composite) [4–6]. Amongst various hybrid supercapacitors, battery-type electrodes gained high interest due to the recent development of lithium-ion capacitors (LIC), which offers some of the advantages of both lithium-ion battery (LIB) and supercapacitor technologies and eliminates their drawbacks. While LiCs represent a promising concept for energy storage, they have one technical difficulty, which is the “pre-lithiation” step which is time-consuming as well as technically challenging [7]. Therefore, alternative battery-type materials are the need of the hour for the development of diverse hybrid charge storage devices.

Although, carbonaceous materials are a contender for high-performance electrode materials for EDLCs because they combine effective conductivity with cheap cost [8–11], recently, a few reports suggested the use of various carbon-based nanomaterials and nanocomposites with unusual battery-type behaviour, which can be highly useful for the next generation solid-state hybrid storage devices. The battery-type behaviour is manifested by various surface-attached moieties that provide redox-active sites for Faradaic reactions [12–14]. Amongst various carbon nanomaterials, C quantum-dots (CQD) are quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterials with very high active surface sites, excellent chemical stability, extremely soluble in water, and have a wide range of functionalization possibilities [15], all of which are very promising attributes for battery-type electrode fabrication [16,17]. Furthermore, the presence of heteroatom-containing functional groups (oxygen, nitrogen, sulphur, phosphorus, boron, and others) on 0D carbon nanomaterials provides a large number of active sites for improved electrochemical performance [18]. Therefore, the CQDs can significantly replace metal-free based electrode materials for energy storage devices. Several recent studies have emphasized the potential of CQDs for enhanced performance in supercapacitors, LIBs, SIBs, metal-air batteries, fuel cells, biofuel cells, electrocatalytic water splitting, CO2 electro-reduction, and electrochemical biosensors using simple approaches at cheap prices [19–23].

In this study, the CQDs were produced from lemon juice using hydrothermal synthesis. The synthesis of CQDs by hydrothermal technique has been converted to a single-step innovative methodology without the need for any precipitating reagent or template. The supercapacitive properties of the S and N CQDs have been examined and compared with the pristine CQDs and to the previous reports. The specific capacity of hetero atom-doped CQDs was much greater than that of pure CQDs, and the paper discusses the synergistic impact of CQDs on specific capacitance improvements. The S-CQDs exhibited superior electrochemical performance with a high specific capacity of 284 mAh/g at 1A/g current density and excellent cycling stability of 94.5% over 10000 GCD cycles.

The importance of the current work lies in the fact that an interesting nanostructure comprising of carbon quantum dots with graphitic structure exhibited an unusual battery-type behaviour (manifested by the presence of a large number of O-/N-/S-containing moieties within the graphitic network to provide very high redox-active sites) rather than the conventional EDLC process (which is mostly observed in various carbonaceous nanostructures), that can be explored for diverse charge storage devices including various types of batteries as well as hybrid supercapacitors for next-generation solid-state energy storage applications. Another importance of the current work is the fabrication of additive-free biomass-derived carbon quantum dots via a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis to produce a CQD-graphene composite electrode for hybrid supercapacitors. Although there are many reports on biomass-derived carbon materials but the reports on the bio-derived CQDs are limited [24–26]. In particular, the fabrication of lemon juice-derived CQDs is very few [27,28]. This is because one needs some encapsulating agents to fabricate zero-dimensional QDs which can impose some constraints on various bottom-up approaches in synthesizing waste-derived CQDs. Indeed, the majority of bio-derived carbonaceous nanomaterials are described to be manufactured by activating agents and/or producing the requisite morphologies with some additives or toxic reagents [29]. While no additives or surfactants are used in the current process of synthesizing CQDs, no harmful reagents are utilized in the post-synthesis steps too, to activate it, thus the process is totally green and the material is eco-friendly and sustainable. Interestingly, there are very few reports (to the best of the Authors’ knowledge), which used a similar method like us to hydrothermally fabricating CQDs from pure lemon-juice [28,30], but, the as-synthesized CQDs are mainly used for optoelectronic applications, and not for charge storage applications, either due to the amorphous structure and/or not having good interconnecting matrix for better charge transfer. In contrast, we have optimized our synthesis technique to get crystalline CQDs (0D) encapsulated with graphene layers (1D), for our target application in energy storage devices. Therefore, our synthesis technique is quite innovative to get a unique, high-crystalline 0D-1D mixed nanocarbon morphology having the requisite properties for high-performance supercapacitors. In addition, a high-performance symmetric supercapacitor device with S-CQDs-graphene composite electrodes is constructed, which demonstrates remarkable charge storage capability. This easy, cost-effective, scalable, and environment-friendly synthesis approach can be used to construct similar eco-friendly carbon-based nanocomposites for a variety of clean energy applications for long-term sustainability.

Lemons were purchased from the local supermarket. Thiourea and melamine were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. For the synthesis of pristine CQDs, a 1:2 ratio of water and lemon juice was transferred into the Teflon-lined autoclave and processed hydrothermally at 200 °C for 6 h. The CQDs were collected by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 6 min and dried in vacuum condition overnight. The remaining liquid has lightweight particles of CQDs and is separated into the glass vial. For the hetero-atom-doped CQDs, melamine (N-doped CQDs) and thiourea (S-doped CQDs) were used. The schematic synthesis process of pristine CQDs, N-CQDs, and S-CQDs is presented in Fig. 1 .
The characterization details and electrochemical measurements are found in the supporting information file.

The XRD patterns of CQDs, N-CQDs, and S-CQDs samples are illustrated in Fig. 2 (a). The XRD patterns revealed a broad diffraction peak, signifying that all as-prepared materials were semi-crystalline. The XRD pattern of CQDs revealed the broad diffraction peaks around 2θ = 20.7°, which corresponds to the (002) plane of CQDs [31]. In N-CQDs and S-CQDs, this peak is observed to be slightly red-shifted (high 2θ-shift), indicating some strain generation within the materials due to the doping of N- and S- heteroatoms within the carbon matrix, [32,33].

The Raman spectra of as-prepared CQDs, N-CQDs, and S-CQDs samples reveal information about the level of graphitic defects and structural disorder in the N and S-CQDs (Fig. 2 (b)). Three characteristic peaks were observed at 1347 cm−1, 1586 cm−1, and 2686 cm−1 corresponding to the D band, G band, and 2D band respectively. The D band corresponds to the Raman active A
1g breathing mode, which originates from the structural disorder within the graphene layers due to the vibrations of carbon atoms with the dangling bonds in the planer termination of disordered graphitic structures. The intensity of this peak depends on several factors including the number of edges, the presence of foreign atoms within the graphene layers, etc. In the present case, the presence of the moderately intense D-peak is mostly due to the incorporation of heteroatoms that creates higher disorder, leading to an increment in the D band intensity [34]. On the other hand, the G band corresponds to the Raman active E
2g breathing mode of interplanar sp 2-bonded C–C stretching vibrations of carbon lattice within the multilayer graphene walls, indicating the formation of well-defined graphitic domains. The higher intensity of the G-band (I
G ) against that of the D-band (I
D ) further corroborates the presence of a well-aligned graphitic network within the undoped and N-/S-doped CQDs. Additionally, the I
D /I
G ratio of the samples is calculated, which depicts I
D /I
G = 0.625 (for CQD), 0.640 (for N-CQD), and 0.676 (for S-CQD), respectively. Evidently, with doping the structural disorder is slightly increasing due to the incorporation of the heteroatoms within the graphitic network. Additionally, the relatively higher I
D /I
G ratio in S-CQDs with respect to N-CQDs is due to the higher atomic radius of S2− (∼0.184 nm) against N-CQDs (∼0.171 nm), which instills a higher change in the lattice spacings, so also, slightly increases the disorder in S-CQDs. Furthermore, the evolution of a 2D-band confirms the formation of the well- and highly-ordered concentric graphene multilayers within carbon nano-onions [35,36]. This well-defined graphitic structure provides very good charge-transfer properties of the CQDs, which have a significant effect on the electrochemical properties of the samples.

The elemental and chemical composition were detected for the as-synthesized CQDs, N-CQDs, and S-CQDs through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Fig. 2(c) shows the survey spectrum, which indicates the presence of elemental peaks of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s. Moreover, the sulphur doped heteroatoms were also detected in the S-CQDs. Fig. 2(d) shows the carbon 1 s deconvoluted spectrum which consists of C–C (284.7 eV), C–O (285.61 eV), CO (287.6 eV), C–N (285.63 eV), and CS (285.65 eV) sub-peaks, respectively, indicating the formation of N and S heteroatoms-doped CQDs. Fig. 2(e) shows the nitrogen 1 s (N 1s) deconvoluted spectrum which consists of pyridinic-N (398.6 eV), Pyrrolic-N (399.9 eV), and graphitic-N (401.5 eV) sub-peaks, indicating the formation of N heteroatoms doped CQDs. These bonding states have a profound effect on the supercapacitive performance of the materials as they provide π-electrons into the carbon network to enhance the redox activity as well as electrical conductivity [35]. As the relative intensity depicts how the electrons are contributed within the network, comparatively higher intensities of pyrrolic- and/or pyridinic-N reveal better redox behavior, whilst graphitic-N represents good charge transport properties [35]. Therefore, the presence of high-intensity pyrrolic-N peaks in our samples indicates excellent redox properties, which led to the unusual battery-type behavior of the electrodes (discussed later). Fig. 2(f) shows the sulphur 2p (S 2p) deconvoluted spectrum which consists of S 2p3/2, S 2p1/2, and SO sub-peaks at respective binding energies of 161.7 eV, 163.7 eV, 165.05 eV respectively, indicating the formation of S heteroatoms doped CQDs. Similarly, Fig. 2(g) represents the oxygen 2 p deconvoluted spectrum consisting of CO (529.5 eV), C–O/C–O–C/O–H (532.2 eV) and surface adsorbed H2O (535.6 eV) sub-peaks indicating bonding with the carbon atoms of CQDs and surface adsorbed hydroxyl groups [37–39].

SEM images of the samples along with EDS mapping are shown in Fig. S1(a)/(b)/(c) (Supplementary Data). The formation of some compact powdered samples is observed. Since the resolution of SEM is not enough to image the CQDs, having a dimension in the nanometer range, this technique is mainly used for elemental analysis of the sample via EDX (whilst microstructural analysis was performed through TEM, shown later). The presence of elemental C, N, and/or S are observed in the respective samples. Also, the elemental mapping, shown in (a-i)/(b-i/ii)/(c-i/ii/iii), reveals the uniform distribution of the elements, indicating homogeneous doping of uniformly distributed CQDs.

The TEM and HR-TEM images in Fig. 3 (a), (b), and (c) display undoped CQDs at three different magnifications, with a size around 5–25 nm, and the lattice spacing was detected at 0.21 nm (inset of (c)), which corresponds to the (100) planes of graphitic carbon. Fig. 3(d)/(e)/(f) and 3(g)/(h)/(i) show the N- and S- heteroatoms doped CQDs, respectively (at three different magnifications), embedded within the graphene layers. CQDs are clearly observed in Fig. 3(a)/d/g, which are enlarged in Fig. 3(b)/e/h, respectively to show the corresponding crystalline planes.

Also, to determine the homogeneity of the CQDs, a TEM image is shown in Fig. S2(a) (Supplementary Data), which depicts well-dispersed CQDs having slight agglomeration, with a relatively narrow size-distribution (inset). The heteroatom-doped CQDs also have similar distribution (not shown here).

Regarding HR-TEM images of doped CQDs, in Fig. 3(f)/(i), two regions are observed with two different lattice spacings. The smaller regions show d = 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) orientation of CQDs, whereas the outer region shows the d-values around 0.34/0.32 nm, which correspond to the (002) planes of graphitic carbon. This indicates the encapsulation of doped CQDs within the graphene network. Interestingly, for doped CQDs, the preferred graphitic lattice orientation changes from [100] (d
100 = 0.21 nm) to [002] (d
002 ∼ 0.336 nm) due to a change in the lattice spacings, which is mainly due to the incorporation of heteroatoms, having ionic radii (N3− ∼ 0.171 nm, S2− ∼ 0.184 nm) comparable to the d-value of the (100) planes (0.210 nm), thus synergistically enhancing the d-spacings of graphitic layer [25,26,40,41]. This is further corroborated by the HR-TEM images of doped CQDs in Figs. S2(b) and (c), where the (100) orientation of CQDs (with a proper lattice spacing of d = 0.21 nm) are clearly visible, which are surrounded by graphene layers having d-spacing of 0.34 nm, that corresponds to the (002) planes of graphitic carbon. Additionally, the FFT images, shown in the corresponding insets of Fig. S2(b)/(c), further confirm the crystalline structure, consisting of the hexagonal unit cell in a honeycomb network, indicating that the integrity of the CQDs is not affected by the heteroatom doping [42]. Further, in the inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) algorithm images (shown in Fig. S2-d/e), the CQDs show some lattice fringes, having an in-plane lattice spacing of 0.23/0.24 (±0.02) nm, which is very close to that of (100) spacing (0.21 nm) of CQDs, indicating a crystalline core of CQDs [43]. In general, carbon dots are considered to be amorphous quasi-spherical nanostructures, whereas Carbon Quantum Dots refer to crystalline spherical carbon nanodots [29]. Therefore, the formation of distinct lattice fringes within our samples clearly confirms the formation of CQDs.

Apparently, the HRTEM images, in combination with Raman and XRD data, indicate that the as-synthesized CQDs are graphitic in nature, having d-spacings well-matched with the graphene layers, and hence, termed graphitic carbon quantum dots. In addition to the highly oriented graphene structures of the samples, the XPS data reveals the addition of π-electrons within the carbon matrix via doping, all of which culminated in excellent charge-transport and redox properties of the samples that led to the superior electrochemical performance of the materials, as discussed below.

The half-cell electrochemical performance of the CQDs, N-CQDs, and S-CQDs nanostructure electrode was examined by cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), which are used broadly to evaluate the electrochemical performance of the as-synthesized electrode material. The CV profiles of the as-prepared CQDs, N-CQDs, and S-CQDs electrodes (@20 mV s−1 scan rate) indicate the battery type feature with one pair of redox peaks appearing as shown in Fig. 4 (a). Fig. 4 (b, c, and d) show the same at various scan rates (10–50 mV s−1). Herein, with an increase in the scan rate, a small peak shift appears that represents the faradaic redox process. At a higher scan rate, CQDs, N-CQDs, and S-CQDs nanostructure electrodes maintained its original shape with a small peak shift indicating the higher capacitive nature and fast ion diffusion nature. In general, carbonaceous electrodes exhibit a quasi-rectangular type EDLC charge storage mechanism, whilst in the current case, an unusual battery-type charge storage mechanism is observed even in the pristine CQDs with strong redox peaks. To verify that this battery-type behavior is manifested due to the sample properties, rather than the substrate effect, we have compared the CV curves of bare Ni foam current collector with the CQD samples (Fig. 4(a)), and it shows negligible current response and, hence, contributes insufficiently towards the overall electrochemical properties of the samples. Therefore, this battery-type behavior is solely originating from the sample properties. Although generally, CQDs, graphene, and its derivatives exhibit the capacitive behavior of an EDLC mechanism, recent literature indicates the use of some of these C-based nanomaterials in battery-type pseudocapacitors too [13,29]. In the current case, the origin of the battery-type behavior in the pristine CQDs is due to the presence of the oxygen and nitrogen moieties within the samples (as indicated by the XPS data) that provide multifold advantages for the faradaic redox process. Firstly, the low-dimensional structure of the CQDs provides a very high specific surface area to manifest a large number of redox-active sites. Secondly, the graphitic network, with well-aligned graphene multilayers, incorporates considerable oxygen and nitrogen functional groups, which further stimulate the reversible redox reactions with the electrolyte ions. Thirdly, the presence of non-hexagonal rings (like anhydride, pyrrole, etc.) as surface functional groups within the hexagonal network of graphitic carbon creates the corrugated structure that further enhances the active surface sites and also reduces the clustering of the foreign atoms to improve the redox activity, and fourthly, these N- and O-containing functional groups improve the hydrophilicity of the electrochemical electrodes which reduces the mass transfer resistance and subsequently enhances the active species for reversible redox reaction with electrolyte ions for battery-type pseudocapacitance. To further corroborate this argument, elemental analyses of the samples are performed and presented in Table 1 . Evidently, undoped and doped CQDs exhibit the presence of a considerable amount of elemental oxygen and nitrogen (as well as sulphur in S-CQDs), indicating their profound effect on the electrochemical properties of the samples.

Regarding N-doped CQDs, the redox peaks become sharper due to the pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen redox reaction with the electrolyte, whilst graphitic N enhances the charge transport properties (as pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, and graphitic-N sub-peaks are observed in the XPS spectra of CQDs, cf. Fig. 2(e)) [35]. Regarding S-CQDs, in this case, also, the redox peaks become sharper due to the promotion of the Faradic reaction between a lone electron pair in the S-based functional groups (like thiophene) and the cations in the electrolyte [44].

In general, a graphitic carbon network contains several functional groups, as proposed in the molecular model presented in the inset of Fig. 4(k). This schematic structure is based on the XPS analysis (cf. Fig. 2), which depicted the probable presence of various carbon bonding including C–C, CC, C–N, CO, CS, pyridinic/pyrrolic/graphitic-N, etc. These functional groups are mainly responsible for the battery-type pseudocapacitive behavior of the samples and also enhance the electronic conductivity of the carbon network to produce excellent electrochemical performance [13]. In the current case (where 6 M KOH solution was used as the active electrolyte), apparently, some of these proposed functional groups, which are acidic in nature in an aqueous solution, would react with the hydroxyl ions (OH−) in the alkaline electrolyte to promote battery-type Faradaic redox reactions [45], as proposed in the Scheme-1 .

Likewise, the galvanostatic charge/discharge analysis also confirms the battery-type behavior through the appearance of the non-linear charge-discharge profile with a potential plateau at redox potential as shown in Fig. 4 (e, f, and g) for as-prepared CQDs, N-CQDs, and S-CQDs nanostructure electrode. Moreover, we observed the decreasing discharge time while increasing the current density due to insufficient time to complete the whole reduction reaction at a higher current density. The specific capacity of the as-prepared CQDs, N-CQDs and S-CQDs nanostructure electrode was calculated from the galvanostatic charge/discharge curve for different current densities as shown in Fig. 4(h). The calculated specific capacity was found to be 284, 181, and 125 mAh/g at current densities of 2 mA for S-CQDs, N-CQDs, and CQDs, respectively, with S-CQDs having superior specific capacitance values for all current densities over other electrodes. Apparently, the presence of both S- and N-containing functional groups manifests superior electrochemical properties of the S-CQDs electrode over the other. Therefore, this electrode is further subjected to the long-term cyclability test, and the corresponding charge-discharge profile at the constant current density of 10 mA for 10,000 cycles is presented in Fig. 4(k). The as-prepared S-CQDs nanostructure electrode displayed brilliant capacitance retention of 94.5% over 10,000 cycles with 98% Coulombic efficiency as shown in Fig. 4(k). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy to analyze the electronic and ionic conductivity of CQDs, N-CQDs, and S-CQDs nanostructure electrodes in the frequency region of 0.1 Hz–1 M Hz as shown in Fig. 4(i). The CQDs, N-CQDs, and S-CQDs nanostructure electrodes show low solution resistance (R
s ) around 1.0 Ω, 0.7 Ω, and 0.5 Ω, indicating the better electrical conductivity as displayed in the inset of Fig. 4(j). In this case, also, the S-CQDs electrode exhibits the lowest R
s , as well as the least charge transfer resistance (R
ct ; indicated by the lowest diameter of distorted semicircular part in the high-frequency region, cf. Fig. 4(j)), and near-ideal capacitive behavior (due to the highest slope of the linear part at mid-to-low frequency region), which are consistent with the GCD and CV data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and testing of symmetric supercapacitor (S-CQDs//S-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The two-electrode symmetric supercapacitors (full-cell) were also fabricated to further examine the practical application of prepared electrode materials. In Fig. 5 (a), a full-cell symmetric supercapacitor device was assembled using as-prepared nanostructured electrodes, with both anode and cathode acting as S-CQDs and KOH-PVA as gel electrolyte. To determine the expected operating potential window, the electrochemical performance of the S-CQDs//S-CQDs symmetric supercapacitor device was tested for different voltages ranging from 0.6 to 1.4 V at a constant scan rate of 20 mV s−1, as shown in Fig. 5(b). The current response remained steady within the voltage range, hence the optimized potential window is set to 1.4 V for subsequent CV measurements. The CV curves of the S-CQDs//S-CQDs symmetric supercapacitor device at different scan rates (10–50 mV s−1), (cf. Fig. 5(c)) confirm the pseudocapacitive behavior of the assembled device. All the CV curves had the same form with no significant differences, indicating that the manufactured device had a high rate-capability and interfacial kinetics. Likewise, the galvanostatic charge/discharge analysis of the S-CQDs//S-CQDs symmetric supercapacitor device was also operated at various voltage ranges between 0.4 V and 1.4 V at a constant current density of 6 Ag-1 (Fig. 5(d)) to determine the optimized potential window for GCD measurements. In this case, also the curves revealed identical features, and hence, the potential window is set to 1.4 V. Next, the GCD curves, shown in Fig. 5(e), confirmed the pseudocapacitive behavior through the appearance of the asymmetric charge/discharge profile with a potential plateau at redox potential for different current densities (0.5–10 A/g).

The fabricated symmetric supercapacitor has high specific capacitance, specific energy, and power densities of 21.7 mAh/g (55.7 F/g) at 1 A/g, 15.2 Wh/kg, and 700 W/kg, respectively (Fig. 5(f and h)). The energy-power performance of the current device is compared with similar devices reported in the literature and presented in Fig. 5(h) [18,36,52–55]. Evidently, our reported solid-state S-CQDs//S-CQDs symmetric supercapacitor device exhibits superior energy-power performance due to the excellent electrochemical properties of the samples with extraordinary battery-type redox behavior.

To further illuminate the kinetics of electron and ion transport within the S-CQDs//S-CQDs symmetric supercapacitor, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted at a frequency ranging from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. The Nyquist plots of the assembled device show a semicircle in the high-frequency region and an approximately vertical line in the low-frequency region, as shown in Fig. 5(g). Furthermore, EIS analysis shows the R
s = 8.2 Ω (cf. inset of Fig. 5(g)), which suggests good ion/electron conductivity for the symmetric supercapacitor device. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 5(i), the hybrid supercapacitor device performed 10,000 continuous charge/discharge cycles at a constant current density of 10 A/g. The fabricated symmetric supercapacitor achieves high capacity retention of 92.5% even after 10,000 cycles. The constructed symmetric supercapacitor device was used in a practical application, where three symmetric supercapacitor devices were connected in series and exhibited a 4.2 V potential window. This device can light up 50 light-emitting diodes (LED) continuously (SYM SUP) for 3 min after charging for 30 s, as shown in the inset of Fig. 5(i).

Therefore, based on the Physico-chemical and electrochemical properties, it can be summarized that the as-fabricated nanomaterials have several interesting aspects, which warrant attention. The unique 0D (CQD)-1D (graphene) mixed nanostructure has several advantages in terms of charge storage application. For example, the 0D CQDs and 1D graphene sheets offer a large number of active sites for improved double-layer capacitance, whereas the doped CQDs, containing a large number of O-/N-/S-containing functional groups, provide very high redox-active sites for unusual battery-type behavior, and the surrounding graphene network instills excellent charge transport properties for better cyclability and rate capability of the hybrid supercapacitor. Additionally, the as-described single-step hydrothermal synthesis of lemon-juice derived CQD-graphene heterostructure neither involves any template or encapsulating agent for dimensional confinement, nor any toxic additive/reagent and/or activating agent is used for producing the requisite morphology, and hence, the process is an innovative yet completely green process. Lastly, in terms of application, the pristine and heteroatom-doped CQD-graphene hybrid electrodes exhibit an unusual battery-type charge storage mechanism with strong redox peaks, manifested due to the presence of various surface moieties, which could open up new possibilities for next-generation solid-state energy storage applications.

In conclusion, the CQDs, N-doped CQDs, and S-doped CQDs electrode materials, encapsulated within the graphene layers, were successfully synthesized by a simple green-hydrothermal method for supercapacitor application. The CQDs were synthesized via a bio-derived sustainable route using lemon juice as the carbon source. The physicochemical properties of prepared electrochemical electrode materials were investigated by XRD, Raman, TEM, and XPS techniques. The electrochemical properties depicted an unusual battery-type behavior, which is manifested due to the presence of a considerable amount of O-/N-/S-containing functional groups within the graphitic network of the CQDs-graphene composite, which enhances the redox-active sites. The remarkable specific capacities were obtained for S-doped CQDs at 284 mAh/g at the current density of 1 A/g and the capacitance retention of 94.5% after 10,000 cycles. For the practical application, a symmetric solid-state supercapacitor device was fabricated by using as-prepared S-doped CQDs (anode and cathode) at an operation potential window of 1.4 V. The assembled symmetric supercapacitor (S-CQDs//S-CQDs) exhibited excellent specific capacitance, specific energy, and power densities corresponding to 21.7 mAh/g (55.7 F/g) at 1 A/g, 15.2 Wh/kg, and 700 W/kg, respectively. In terms of the practical applicability of the device, three symmetric supercapacitors were coupled in series, and this device demonstrated a potential window of 4.2 V, capable of constantly lighting 50 light-emitting diodes (LED) for 3 min (SYM SUP) after charging for 30 s.

Mohan Reddy Pallavolu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Visualization, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. S. Prabhu: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ramesh Reddy Nallapureddy: Data curation, Software, Formal analysis. Arla Sai Kumar: Software, Formal analysis. Arghya Narayan Banerjee: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sang Woo Joo: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This Grant (NRF-2019R1A5A8080290) from the National Research Foundation of Korea is supporting this study.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2022.10.077.
